Main hazards: Floods, river erosion, cyclones, landslides, drought
Examples of high‑risk districts / cities: Brahmaputra valley districts such as Barpeta, Darrang, Dhemaji, Goalpara, Golaghat, Sivasagar; dual flood–drought risk in Charaideo, Dibrugarh, Sibsagar, South Salmara‑Mankachar, Golaghat.pib.gov+2

Main hazards: Severe river floods, drought, heat stress
Examples of high‑risk districts / cities: Sitamarhi, Madhubani, Supaul, Kishanganj, Katihar, Saharsa, Darbhanga, Lakhisarai, Nalanda, Patna; dual flood–drought risk in Patna and several north Bihar districts.pib.gov+2

Main hazards: Floods in Indo‑Gangetic plains, drought in plateau pockets
Examples of high‑risk districts / cities: Flood‑prone: Paschim Champaran–adjacent Ganga plains; drought hotspots include Jhansi, Lalitpur, Banda, Mirzapur and neighbouring districts; multiple districts in “high drought risk” category.geographynotes+2

Main hazards: Floods, cyclones, river erosion, drought pockets
Examples of high‑risk districts / cities: Murshidabad, Malda, Nadia, North & South 24 Parganas (flood and cyclone risk); Purulia (drought pocket); dual flood–drought risk in Murshidabad, Nadia, Uttar Dinajpur.jhanjhar+2
Coastal districts Puri, Kendrapara, Bhadrak, Balasore (cyclone + flood); inland drought‑prone pockets include Mayurbhanj, Kalahandi and parts of Balasore.jhanjhar+2

Kutch and Saurashtra region (Kutch, Jamnagar, Rajkot, Morbi, Junagadh, Devbhoomi Dwarka) are major drought and dryness hotspots; Rajkot and Surendranagar identified as districts shifting from flood to drought risk.byjus+2

Main hazards: Extreme drought, heat waves, dust storms
Examples of high‑risk districts / cities: Western desert and semi‑arid districts including Bikaner, Jaisalmer, Barmer, Jodhpur, Churu, Ajmer, Jalor, Nagaur, Ganganagar, Hanumangarh; several listed among India’s most drought‑vulnerable.geographynotes+2
Drought‑hotspot districts such as Ahmednagar, Aurangabad, Nanded, Beed; Marathwada and Vidarbha regions broadly; flood‑drought hotspot Aurangabad and several central districts.indianexpress+2

North‑west and central MP included in severe and moderate drought zones; districts like Rajkot‑linked drought hotspots in regional studies; many districts marked “high” in climate‑risk vulnerability lists.geographynotes+2
North Karnataka districts such as Bagalkot, Bijapur (Vijayapura), Gulbarga (Kalaburagi), Hassan and adjoining areas are highlighted as drought‑affected hotspots.indianexpress+2
Khammam and Mahabubnagar repeatedly cited as major drought‑prone districts; Mahbubnagar and Nalgonda also show shifting from flood to drought in recent decades.byjus+2

Coastal flood‑prone districts such as Guntur, Kurnool, Srikakulam and others; inland drought hotspots like Anantapur and Chittoor (Rayalaseema); coastal districts also exposed to cyclones.indianexpress+2

Coastal Chennai and nearby districts (cyclone and flood hotspot); interior drought pockets including Coimbatore, Tiruchirappalli and Tirunelveli; districts simultaneously facing flood and drought in recent assessments.geographynotes+2

Main hazards: Extreme rainfall, floods, landslides, dual flood–drought
Examples of high‑risk districts / cities: Alappuzha listed as very high risk for both flood and drought; landslide‑prone hill districts such as Idukki, Wayanad, and parts of Kollam, Ernakulam; multiple coastal districts marked highly vulnerable in NICRA list.deccanherald+2

Palamau identified as a drought pocket; districts like Garhwa, Godda, Sahibganj, Pakur and West Singhbhum listed as “very high” climate‑vulnerable; some eastern districts face flood risk along Ganga tributaries.geographynotes+2
Jashpur, Raigarh, Korba, Mahasamund, Kanker and other districts categorized as vulnerable to climate‑driven drought and hydro‑meteorological hazards.pib.gov+1
Fatehabad, Bhiwani, Mahendragarh listed as very high vulnerability districts; several others (Kaithal, Jind, Sirsa, Hisar, Rohtak etc.) in high‑vulnerability category for climate‑risk.pib.gov+1

Main hazards: Drought, groundwater stress, some flood risk
Examples of high‑risk districts / cities: Gurdaspur, Jalandhar, Moga, Faridkot, Bathinda and others are identified as vulnerable in NICRA’s climate‑risk categorization, with issues of water stress and changing rainfall.pib.gov+1
High seismic and landslide risk in districts like Srinagar, Baramulla, Anantnag; Leh and Kargil (Ladakh) marked highly vulnerable; flood‑prone pockets along Jhelum and other rivers.pib.gov+2
Chamba and Mandi listed as very high vulnerability districts; Kangra, Kullu, Hamirpur, Bilaspur, Shimla, Kinnaur also highly vulnerable to combined earthquake–landslide–flood risks.pib.gov+2

Rudraprayag, Pithoragarh, Chamoli, Uttarkashi mapped as the most sensitive districts for earthquakes and landslides; Uttarkashi, Tehri Garhwal, Bageshwar, Almora, Champawat also in very high vulnerability lists.indiatoday+2
North, West, East and South Sikkim districts classified as very‑high or high vulnerability; Gangtok and other hill towns are in high seismic and landslide risk zones.pib.gov+2

Upper Siang (very high vulnerability) and districts like Lower Subansiri, Tawang, West & East Siang, Lohit, Dibang Valley listed as highly vulnerable in climate‑risk and landslide assessments.pib.gov+2

State / UT: Nagaland, Manipur, Mizoram, Meghalaya, Tripura (NE hill states)
Examples of high‑risk districts / cities: Hill districts across these states (e.g., Senapati, Churachandpur, Ukhrul, Chandel; various Garo/Khasi/Jaintia Hills; Lawngtlai, Saiha, Aizawl, Champhai etc.) are categorized as high vulnerability for earthquakes, landslides and heavy rainfall‑driven floods.pib.gov+2
Port Blair and neighbouring islands fall in India’s highest seismic zone and are exposed to tsunamis and cyclones from the Bay of Bengal.pib.gov+2

Coastal districts are noted in hazard maps for extreme rainfall, localized flooding and shoreline erosion, though overall vulnerability is lower compared to major flood/drought belts.geoportal.natmo.gov+1
